cues.x: Let me start with a story you have probably already heard, which is exactly the point.
A man is traveling for business.
He meets an attractive woman at a hotel bar.
She offers to buy him a drink.
He accepts, takes a few sips, and then everything goes hazy.
The next thing he remembers is waking up in a bathtub full of ice, in his own hotel room, with a strange stinging pain in his lower back and a note taped to the wall next to him.
The note says, call 911 or you will die.
There is a phone resting on a small table beside the tub.
He dials with numb, shaking fingers.
The dispatcher seems oddly familiar with his situation.
She tells him to reach back slowly and feel if there is a tube protruding from his lower back.
There is.
Do not move, she says.
We are sending help.
You have had one of your kidneys removed.
There is a ring of organ thieves working the city.
That story is not true.
It never happened.
And yet you have almost certainly heard some version of it, and you remember it, and if I asked you to retell it tonight at dinner you could do it without breaking a sweat.
That is what this book is about.
It is called Made to Stick, Why Some Ideas Survive and Others Die, written by two brothers, Chip Heath and Dan Heath.
Chip is a professor at Stanford, Dan works in education and writing, and together they set out to answer a question that quietly runs your entire life whether you notice it or not.

Why do some ideas, like the kidney heist, spread on their own, stick in your memory for years, and even change how you behave, while other ideas, often far more important and far more true, sink without a trace the moment they leave your mouth.
Why can a random urban legend outlive a company's carefully crafted mission statement.
Why does a false story beat a true one.
And most importantly, since you almost certainly have ideas you need other people to understand and remember and act on, how do you make your own ideas stick.
So here is the promise of the next forty five minutes or so.
You are going to learn that stickiness is not a magic gift some people are born with.
It is not charisma.
It is not luck.
It is a set of traits that sticky ideas share, and once you can see those traits, you can build them into your own ideas on purpose.

The Heaths studied urban legends, they studied great advertising, they studied teachers who change lives and generals who win battles and the kind of proverbs that survive for thousands of years, and they found the same handful of principles showing up again and again.
But before we get to those principles, you have to meet the villain of the whole book.
Because there is a villain, and it is probably hiding inside your own head right now.
The villain is called the Curse of Knowledge.
Here is the idea in one sentence.
Once you know something, you cannot imagine what it was like not to know it.
That sounds harmless.
It is not.
It is the single biggest reason smart, well meaning, expert people are terrible at communicating what they know.

Let me give you the experiment that proves it, because it is one of the most quietly devastating experiments in psychology, and it comes from a Stanford graduate student named Elizabeth Newton in 1990.
Newton set up a simple game with two roles.
There were tappers and there were listeners.
Each tapper got a list of famous, obvious songs, the kind everyone knows, Happy Birthday, the national anthem, that sort of thing.
The tapper picked a song and tapped out the rhythm on a table with a finger.
The listener's job was to guess the song from the tapping.
Simple.
Now, before the listener guessed, Newton asked each tapper to predict how likely it was that the listener would get it right.
The tappers were confident.
On average they predicted that the listener would guess the song about half the time, fifty percent.
Fifty percent.
In reality, out of a hundred and twenty songs that got tapped out, the listeners guessed correctly two and a half percent of the time.
Not fifty.
Two and a half.
Sit with that gap for a second, because that gap is the whole book.
Why were the tappers so wildly, absurdly wrong about how obvious they were being.
Because when a tapper taps out Happy Birthday, they cannot help but hear the full song playing in their head.
The melody, the words, the whole rich thing.
But the listener does not hear any of that.
The listener hears a bunch of disconnected taps, like a strange kind of Morse code.
The tapper is astonished that anyone could fail to hear something so obvious.
But it is only obvious to them, because they have information the listener does not have, and once you have that information, you literally cannot un know it.
You cannot get back to the innocent state of not knowing.
Now here is why this matters so much for you.
Every time you try to explain your business, your project, your idea, your cause, you are the tapper.
You have the song playing in your head.
You know the strategy, you know the context, you know why it matters, you have been thinking about it for months.
And the person listening to you is just hearing taps.
They do not have the song.

And the Curse of Knowledge makes you feel like you are being perfectly clear when you are not, and worse, it makes you impatient and frustrated with the listener, as if the failure to understand is their fault.
It is not.
It is yours, and it is fixable, but only if you accept that you are cursed.
The Heaths say there are really only two ways to beat the Curse of Knowledge.
One, do not learn anything, which is obviously a terrible plan.
Two, transform your ideas using the principles in this book.
So that is where we are headed.
The Heaths noticed that sticky ideas, whether they were urban legends or brilliant ad campaigns or ancient proverbs, tended to share six traits.
And in a very sticky move of their own, they arranged those six traits into an acronym.
The acronym is SUCCESs, spelled with the six principles as the letters.
Simple.
Unexpected.
Concrete.
Credible.
Emotional.
Stories.
Simple, Unexpected, Concrete, Credible, Emotional, Stories.

That spells SUCCES, and they added an extra s on the end to make it a real word, and they will be the first to admit that is a bit of a cheat, but it works, and it is memorable, which is exactly the point of the whole book.
Let me walk you through all six, deeply, one at a time, with the real examples they use.
And keep the tappers in mind as we go, because the Curse of Knowledge is the enemy at every single step.
Let us start with the first one.
Simple.
Now, when the Heaths say simple, they do not mean dumbing down, and they do not mean short for the sake of short.
What they mean is finding the core of your idea, the one thing that matters most, and being ruthless about it.
Simple equals core plus compact.
First you find the core, then you make it compact enough to stick.
The clearest example they give for finding the core comes from the United States Army, of all places.
The Army spent years perfecting elaborate battle plans, detailed instructions for exactly what every unit should do.
And they learned a painful lesson, which is that no plan survives contact with the enemy.
The moment the battle starts, everything changes, the enemy does something unexpected, the weather turns, a bridge is out, and the beautiful detailed plan is worthless.
So the Army developed a concept called Commander's Intent.
At the top of every order, in plain language, they state the single most important goal of the operation.
Not the how, the what and the why.
Something like, break the will of the enemy in the region, or, take that hill so we control the valley.
The Commander's Intent is the core.
If soldiers get cut off and the plan falls apart, they still know the one thing that matters, and they can improvise toward it.
That is what a simple idea does for the people carrying it.
It lets them make good decisions when you are not there to tell them what to do.
Think about how that applies to you.
If you run a team, or a family, or a project, you cannot script every situation.
But if you can hand people the core, the Commander's Intent, they can figure out the rest.
Southwest Airlines did exactly this.
For decades their people made thousands of small decisions guided by one core idea.
They are THE low fare airline.
Not a low fare airline, THE low fare airline.
The Heaths tell the story of an executive named Herb Kelleher who supposedly explained it like this.
If someone in marketing suggests adding a fancier chicken Caesar salad to the flights because customers might like it, the question is simple.
Will adding that salad make us THE low fare airline.
No.
It will not.
So we do not add the salad.
That one core idea lets everyone in the company make the salad decision, and a thousand other decisions, without asking the boss.
That is the power of finding the core.
But finding the core is only half.
You also have to make it compact, and this is where a lot of good ideas die.
The Heaths point to the world of newspapers for a lesson called burying the lead.
Journalists are taught something called the inverted pyramid.
You put the single most important information in the very first sentence, the lead, and then you arrange everything else in descending order of importance.
Why.
Partly so that if an editor needs to cut the story to fit the page, they can chop from the bottom without losing the essential point.
The opposite of this is burying the lead, where you save the most important fact for the end, or drown it in detail, and the reader gives up before they reach it.

There is a wonderful teaching story in the book about a journalism instructor, sometimes told as Nora Ephron's teacher, who gives the class a set of facts about a high school and asks them to write the lead.
The facts are that the principal announced all the teachers will travel to Sacramento next Thursday for a seminar featuring various famous speakers.
The students dutifully write leads summarizing the seminar.
And the teacher tells them they all missed it.
The lead is, there will be no school next Thursday.
That is what actually matters to a reader.
Finding the lead is finding the core.
It is a discipline of asking, of everything I could say, what is the one thing.
The Heaths also talk about a small town newspaper editor who was asked what makes his paper successful, and his answer was, names, names, and names.
If it takes a whole extra section and more reporters to get more names of local people into the paper, he will do it, because that is the core of what a local paper is for.
He knows his one thing.
Now, once you have your compact core, how do you pack a lot of meaning into a few words.
The Heaths' answer is schemas and analogies.
A schema is a concept you already have in your head, and a good analogy lets you borrow one to explain something new almost instantly.
Their favorite example is the pomelo.
Imagine I try to explain what a pomelo is by giving you a botanical description.
It is the largest citrus fruit, the rind is thick but soft and easy to peel, and so on.
Your eyes glaze over.
Now imagine I just say, a pomelo is basically a supersized grapefruit with a very thick, soft rind.
Done.
You get it instantly, because you already have a grapefruit schema in your head and I just told you how to modify it.
That is a high concept pitch.
Hollywood does this constantly.
Alien was pitched as Jaws on a spaceship.
A new company gets described as the Uber of dog walking, or the Disney of some other industry.
When you say a company wants to be the Disney of X, everyone immediately knows you mean magical, meticulous, family friendly, premium experience, without you having to spell any of it out.
You are borrowing an existing schema.
That is what makes an idea compact and portable.
So principle one, simple, is find the core, and share it in a form that fits in a person's head.
Now the second principle.
Unexpected.
Because a simple idea is useless if nobody pays attention to it in the first place.
You have two jobs here, and they are different jobs.
First you have to grab attention.
Then you have to hold it.
To grab attention, you break a pattern.
Human beings are wired to notice change and to tune out anything constant and predictable.
Your brain is a pattern machine, and it stops paying attention the moment it thinks it knows what comes next.
So to grab someone, you violate their expectations.
You surprise them.
The Heaths give a great advertising example, a kind of quiz.
They describe several car commercials and ask which one is the surprising Saturn ad.
Most car ads are the same, a gleaming car winding along a mountain road, a deep voice talking about torque and horsepower and zero to sixty.
Predictable.
You have seen it a thousand times, and you ignore it.
The surprising ad breaks the pattern by not being about the machine at all, but about the relationship between the company and the person, which was Saturn's whole different pitch.
When an ad refuses to do the thing every other ad in its category does, you actually notice.
Their sharpest example of pattern breaking is a set of airplane safety announcements.
You know the drill.
In the event of a loss of cabin pressure, an oxygen mask will drop from the panel above you.
You have heard it so many times that your brain has filed it under ignore completely.
Flight attendants can barely get anyone to look up.
But some airlines started writing funny, surprising safety announcements.

One flight attendant, instead of the usual script, said something like, in the event of a water landing, your seat cushion can be used as a flotation device, and then added, in the unlikely event that we land on water, please note that the government has never actually explained how a lake ended up under our flight path.
People laughed.
And crucially, people listened, because the pattern was broken.
The information was the same, but the surprise made people pay attention to information they had ignored a thousand times.
And there is a famous style of surprise the Heaths celebrate, the kind where a statement flips your understanding of a whole system.

Picture an ad that says, there are more nerve endings in the human brain than there are, and then names some vast number, and uses that to make a point about, say, the power of the mind or the importance of not damaging it.
The point is that a well placed astonishing fact, delivered at the right moment, cracks your complacency open.
Surprise makes you stop, and it makes you rethink.
But here is the subtle part.
Surprise gets attention for a moment.
It does not hold attention.
A shocking fact wears off.
To hold attention over time, you do not use surprise, you use curiosity.
And the Heaths' theory of curiosity comes from an economist named George Loewenstein, and it is called the gap theory of curiosity.
Loewenstein said curiosity is what happens when you feel a gap in your knowledge.
When you become aware that there is something you do not know, and specifically something you want to know, that gap is like an itch, and you cannot rest until you scratch it by closing the gap.
Curiosity, in this view, is not about pouring in facts.
It is the opposite.
It is about opening a gap first, making the person feel the absence of a piece of knowledge, so that they lean in wanting it.
The company that mastered this, the Heaths say, is Nordstrom, and also Hollywood.
Think about a movie trailer.
A good trailer does not tell you the whole story.
It shows you just enough to open a giant question.
Who is that man.
What is in the box.
Will she make it out.
The trailer creates a knowledge gap, and the only way to close it is to buy a ticket.
Mystery novels do the same thing, so do good teachers.
A great teacher does not start by dumping the answer on you.
They start by posing a puzzle, by making you realize you do not know something you suddenly want to know.

The Heaths tell of a colleague who starts a class on the search for extraterrestrial life not with facts, but with a question about how many advanced civilizations there might be in the galaxy, walking students through each unknown so they feel every gap.
And there is a story about the broadcasting genius Roone Arledge, who transformed televised sports.
Sports on TV used to just show you the game, a camera pointing at a field.
Arledge understood that most viewers did not know or care about the two random teams playing.
So he built up the stories, the rivalries, the personal dramas, the human stakes, before and during the game.
He gave you a reason to care, he opened gaps you wanted closed, so that a game between strangers became must watch television.
So principle two, unexpected, is break a pattern to grab attention, then open a curiosity gap to hold it.
On to the third principle, and it might be the most useful one for most people, because most of us are terrible at it.
Concrete.
Make your ideas concrete.
Sensory.
Something a person can see, touch, hear, do.
The enemy of concrete is abstraction, and abstraction is the natural language of experts, which brings the Curse of Knowledge roaring back.
The more you know about something, the more you talk about it in abstract, high level terms, and the more you lose the people listening.

The Heaths reach all the way back to Aesop for this one, because his fables are two and a half thousand years old and we still remember them, which is stickiness on a scale most of us can only dream of.
Think of the fox and the grapes.
A hungry fox sees a beautiful bunch of grapes hanging high on a vine.
He jumps and jumps and cannot reach them.
Finally he gives up and walks away, muttering that the grapes were probably sour anyway.
From that little concrete story we get the phrase sour grapes, and everyone knows exactly what it means.
Now imagine Aesop had tried to teach the same lesson abstractly.
He might have said, human beings tend to disparage things they cannot attain in order to protect their egos.
True.
Accurate.
Utterly forgettable.
The concrete story of a fox and some grapes has outlived every abstract phrasing by two millennia.
That is the power of concrete.
Concreteness also helps you do things, not just remember them.
The Heaths tell the story of how Boeing designed the 727 airplane.
It was a massively complicated project, thousands of engineers, endless technical decisions.
Instead of a vague goal like build a great new airplane, the managers gave the team a concrete target.
The 727 must seat one hundred and thirty one passengers, fly nonstop from Miami to New York City, and land on a specific runway at LaGuardia that was too short for existing jets.
That concreteness did something magical.

An engineer working on one tiny subsystem could look at that goal and know whether their piece was on track, and could coordinate with everyone else, because they were all aiming at the same vivid, specific picture.
Concreteness lets people coordinate.
When a goal is abstract, everyone imagines something slightly different, and the pieces do not fit.
When it is concrete, everyone is building the same thing.
The Heaths offer a lovely image for why concrete ideas stick in memory.
They call it the Velcro theory of memory.
Velcro works because one side has thousands of tiny hooks and the other side has thousands of tiny loops, and the more hooks that catch loops, the stronger the bond.
Your memory works similarly.
An idea sticks when it hooks into lots of things you already know.
Concrete, sensory ideas have more hooks.
When I say bicycle, you can picture one, feel the handlebars, remember riding one, smell the summer air, and all of those are hooks catching into your existing memory.
When I say economic justice, there is almost nothing to grab onto, no hooks, so the idea slides right off.
The more concrete you make something, the more hooks it grows.
They tell a story about teaching accounting, of all the dry subjects, through concreteness.

A professor realized students found abstract accounting principles impossible to grasp, so instead of teaching the rules first, he had them run a tiny concrete business, buying and selling a real product, and the abstract principles suddenly made sense because they were attached to something the students had actually done with their hands.

They also describe a nature center where the guides learned that reciting abstract facts about the ecosystem bored visitors, but pointing to a specific animal, a specific track in the mud, a specific thing you could look at and touch, brought the whole thing alive.
So principle three, concrete, means say it in a way a person can picture and act on, because concrete is memorable, concrete coordinates people, and abstraction is the language of the cursed expert.
Now the fourth principle.
Credible.
Because it is not enough for an idea to be understood and remembered.
People also have to believe it enough to act on it.
And how do you make people believe.
The obvious answer is authority.
You cite an expert, a scientist, a study, a famous name, and that borrowed authority makes your claim believable.
That works, and it is worth using.
But the Heaths point out something surprising, which is that anti authorities can be even more powerful than authorities.
An anti authority is someone whose credibility comes not from a title but from hard won, painful, personal experience.
Their prime example is a woman named Pam Laffin.
Pam was featured in a series of anti smoking ads.
She was not a doctor or a surgeon general.
She was a young mother, dying of emphysema from smoking, and she let the cameras show her, the oxygen tubes, the surgical scars, the way the disease had aged and ravaged her body.
Nobody watching doubted her credibility, because her authority came from her suffering.
She was living proof.
That is an anti authority, and sometimes the honest voice of someone who has been through the thing is more convincing than any expert.
The second tool for credibility is vivid detail.
This is a little counterintuitive.
You would think detail is just decoration, but concrete, vivid details actually make people believe you, because they signal that you really know what you are talking about, that you were there.

The Heaths cite research where a description of a defendant that included small, vivid, almost irrelevant details, like the fact that a woman had a Star Wars toothbrush for her son, made juries more likely to believe the account, even though the toothbrush had nothing to do with guilt or innocence.
The specificity itself reads as truth.
The third tool is one you have to be careful with.
Statistics.
Numbers are the natural tool of the credible expert, and they are usually deadly boring and totally unpersuasive, because a raw statistic is abstract and has no hooks.
The Heaths' rule is that statistics work only when you make them human scaled, when you translate them into something a person can feel.
Their favorite example comes from Stephen Covey, who wanted to describe how little most organizations pull together toward a shared goal.

Instead of citing survey percentages, he said, imagine a soccer team where only four of the eleven players know which goal is theirs, only two of them care whether the team wins, and most of them are, in some sense, competing against their own teammates rather than the other team.
Suddenly you feel the dysfunction.
The Heaths also point at the challenge of grasping a huge number, like a trillion.
A trillion is just a word, it means nothing.

But if you say a million seconds is about eleven and a half days, while a billion seconds is nearly thirty two years, and a trillion seconds is over thirty one thousand years, now you feel the scale, now the number means something.
Statistics are not about the number, they are about the sense of scale and relationship the number creates.
Use them to illustrate, not to prove.
Then there is one of my favorite concepts in the whole book, the Sinatra Test.
It comes from the Frank Sinatra song about New York, the line that goes, if I can make it there, I will make it anywhere.
A single example passes the Sinatra Test when it is so impressive that it proves you can handle anything.
The Heaths tell of a small security company trying to win business, and their killer credential was that they handled security for a certain famous, high stakes client.
If you can guard that, the reasoning goes, you can guard anything.
One example, chosen well, does all the work of a hundred testimonials.
And finally, the most powerful credibility tool of all, because it does not require the audience to trust you at all.
Testable credentials.
You invite people to test the claim themselves.
The classic example is the Wendy's ad campaign, where a little old lady looks at a competitor's giant bun with a tiny patty and demands, where's the beef.
That single testable question invited every customer to go look at a competitor's burger and check for themselves.
And it did.
The other great example is political.
In a presidential debate, Ronald Reagan did not try to argue about economic policy statistics with his opponent.
He turned to the audience and asked a single testable question.
Are you better off than you were four years ago.
He let every voter run the test in their own life.
That is the strongest credibility there is, the kind the listener verifies themselves.

So principle four, credible, means help people believe, through authorities and anti authorities, through vivid detail, through human scaled statistics, through one Sinatra example, and best of all, through a claim people can test for themselves.
Now the fifth principle, and this is the one that turns understanding into action.
Emotional.
Make people care.
Because people do not act on ideas they merely believe.
They act on ideas they feel.
And the central, almost shocking lesson of this chapter is the power of one individual over statistics.
We are wired to feel for a person, a face, a name, and we go numb in front of numbers, even when the numbers describe unimaginable suffering.
The Heaths lean on Mother Teresa, who reportedly said, if I look at the mass, I will never act.
If I look at the one, I will.
That is the whole principle in one line.
But they also cite a piece of hard research that proves it, an experiment by a scholar named Deborah Small and colleagues.
Researchers gave people a chance to donate to fight hunger in Africa.
One group got a page of statistics.
Millions of people are starving, food shortages affect this many countries, rainfall deficits have caused this percentage of crop failure.
All true, all staggering.
The other group got a story about a single girl.
Her name is Rokia, she is seven years old, she lives in Mali, she is desperately poor and faces the threat of severe hunger, and your donation will change her life.
Same cause.
The people who read about Rokia gave more than twice as much as the people who saw the statistics.
The one girl beat the millions.
And then the researchers did something diabolical.
They gave a third group both.
They gave them Rokia's story and the statistics together.
And you would think that would raise donations even higher, story plus scale.
It did not.
Adding the statistics to Rokia's story actually reduced giving.
Why.
Because the moment you introduce statistics, you nudge people into an analytical, calculating frame of mind, and that analytical mindset switches off the emotional, caring response.
The numbers made people cooler, more rational, and less generous.
This is called the identifiable victim effect, and it should change how you ask anyone for anything.
If you want people to care, give them one person to care about, and be careful about drowning that person in data.
The next tool for making people care is appealing to self interest, but doing it right.
Self interest works, obviously.
Tell people how something benefits them and they lean in.
But the Heaths point out we usually do this badly, in a flat, abstract way.
The vivid way is to let people picture themselves inside the benefit.
And they add a crucial twist, which is that appealing to identity can be even stronger than appealing to self interest.
People do not just ask, what is in it for me.
They ask, what would a person like me do here.
Their beautiful example is an anti littering campaign in Texas.
For years the state had tried to reduce littering on highways with the usual approach, gentle appeals to civic duty, please keep our state clean, do the right thing.
It did not work, especially not on the group doing most of the littering, young men in pickup trucks who did not care one bit about tidy roadsides or being good citizens.
So the campaign changed strategy completely.
Instead of appealing to duty, they appealed to Texan identity, to toughness and pride.
They created the slogan Don't Mess with Texas, they put famous tough Texans, football players and musicians, on television crushing beer cans and daring anyone to litter in their state.
And it worked spectacularly.
Littering dropped dramatically.
The identity appeal, this is who we are and we do not do that, beat every polite appeal to civic duty, because it spoke to how those men saw themselves.

And there is one more idea in this chapter you should hold onto, drawn from Maslow's famous hierarchy of needs, the pyramid that runs from basic physical survival at the bottom up through safety, belonging, esteem, and self actualization at the top.
The Heaths point out a strange thing about human nature.
When we think about what motivates other people, we assume they care about the low, selfish stuff, money, security, comfort.
But when we think about what motivates ourselves, we believe we care about the higher stuff, meaning, growth, doing something that matters.
We systematically underestimate other people's higher motives.
So when you try to persuade someone, you tend to pitch to the bottom of the pyramid, the money, when they might actually be moved by the top, the meaning.
Do not assume everyone else is only in it for the paycheck.
Give people a chance to be their higher selves.

So principle five, emotional, means make people care by focusing on one identifiable individual rather than statistics, by appealing to self interest and especially identity, and by remembering that people are often moved by more than you assume.
And now the sixth and final principle, the one that ties everything together.
Stories.
Because a great story is not just one of the six principles, it is often a delivery system for all the others at once.
Stories are naturally concrete, they usually carry emotion, they often contain the unexpected, and they can be simple and credible all at the same time.
A story is a stickiness machine.
The Heaths make a distinction that is worth carrying with you.
Stories do two things.
They act as simulation, teaching you how to act, and they act as inspiration, giving you the energy to act.
Let me take those one at a time.
First, simulation.
When you hear a story, your mind does not just record it, it runs it.
You mentally simulate the events, you put yourself in the shoes of the person in the story, you rehearse the situation.
And here is the astonishing research the Heaths cite.
Mental simulation is almost as effective as physical practice.
Studies of people rehearsing a skill purely in their heads, imagining the movements in vivid detail, showed real improvement, nearly as much as people who physically practiced.

So when you tell someone a story about how a problem was solved, you are not just informing them, you are letting them rehearse the solution, so that when they face something similar, they have already, in a sense, been there.
Their example of this is beautiful and comes from a study by an anthropologist named Julian Orr who observed Xerox repair technicians.
The company thought the technicians learned from manuals.
They did not.
The real knowledge, the hard won tricks for fixing stubborn machines, got passed around over breakfast and coffee, as the technicians swapped war stories about strange breakdowns they had conquered.
A story about a machine that failed in a bizarre way, and the clever thing one technician tried, taught the others more than any manual, because it let them mentally simulate the fix.
When Xerox finally understood this, they built a system to collect and share those stories, and it made the whole workforce better.
Second, inspiration.
Stories give people the will to act, often by showing them that change is possible.
The Heaths borrow a wonderful idea here from a man named Stephen Denning, who worked at the World Bank.
Denning was trying to convince a huge, bureaucratic, skeptical organization to embrace a new way of sharing knowledge across the world.
He tried the usual thing, charts, logic, projections, and everyone's eyes glazed over.
Then he told a small, true story.

He told them about a health worker in a remote town in Zambia, in 1995, who needed to know how to treat malaria, and who found the answer he needed on a website from the Centers for Disease Control, thousands of miles away.
That was it.
A tiny story.
But it did something the charts could not.
It let the listeners picture their own organization doing that, connecting knowledge across the world.
Denning called this a springboard story, a story that springs people from where they are now to imagining what could be.
It does not spell out the whole plan.
It plants a single vivid example that lets people leap to the possibility on their own.
And that leap, that self generated vision, is far more motivating than any argument you could make for them.
The most famous story example in the whole book is Jared, the Subway guy.

This is the man who was severely overweight, over four hundred pounds, and who lost an enormous amount of weight, hundreds of pounds, largely by eating Subway sandwiches every day as part of his own homemade diet.
Subway did not invent this.
Jared did it on his own, a local paper wrote about him, and eventually Subway, somewhat nervously, built an ad campaign around his story.
And it was a monster success.
Now, why did the Jared story work so much better than the obvious alternative, which would have been to advertise that Subway offers seven sandwiches with under six grams of fat.
Look at how many of the six principles the Jared story hits at once, all on its own.
It is simple, eat fresh and you can be healthy.
It is unexpected, you can lose that much weight eating fast food, which breaks every pattern you expect.
It is concrete, a real man, a real pair of enormous old pants he holds up, real sandwiches.
It is credible, a testable, verifiable person who actually did it.
It is emotional, you feel for Jared, one identifiable person, not a nutrition statistic.
And it is a story, so it acts as both simulation, here is how someone did it, and inspiration, if he can do it, maybe I can.
Subway did not have to engineer those six things one by one.
A great true story delivered all of them together.
That is why the Heaths save stories for last.
Find the right story, and you get the whole framework in a single package.
So there they are, all six.
Simple, find the core and make it compact.
Unexpected, break a pattern to grab attention and open a gap to hold it.
Concrete, make it something a person can see and touch and do.
Credible, help people believe it, especially in ways they can test themselves.
Emotional, make people care by focusing on one, not on statistics.
And Stories, which let people simulate and inspire themselves into action.
SUCCESs.

And through every single one of them runs the same enemy, the Curse of Knowledge, that maddening fact that once you know your own idea deeply, you lose the ability to feel how it lands on someone who does not.
Every principle is really a tool for climbing back out of your own head and into the head of the tapper's listener, who is only hearing the taps.
Now, I want to make sure you walk away with the deepest, most freeing idea in this entire book, because it is easy to miss under all the examples.
The Heaths are adamant about this.
Stickiness is not a gift.
You do not have to be born creative, or charismatic, or a natural storyteller.
That is a myth, and it is a convenient one, because it lets you off the hook.
If sticky ideas come from a magic spark you either have or do not, then there is nothing to learn.
But that is not what the Heaths found.
They found that sticky ideas share a small, definable set of traits, and that those traits can be studied, learned, and deliberately built into your own ideas, one by one, like a checklist.
In fact, they point out that the most creative ad campaigns, when analyzed, tend to use the same handful of basic templates, including the pattern of surprise we talked about earlier.
Creativity, at least the useful, sticky kind, is far more systematic than it looks.
You are not waiting for lightning.
You are running a process.

So let me leave you with something concrete to do, because we just spent forty five minutes on the principle that concrete beats abstract, and I am not going to leave you with a vague feeling of motivation that fades by the time you finish your drive or your workout.
Here is your this week action list.
Pick one idea that actually matters to you right now.
Something you need other people to get, and remember, and act on.
It could be a pitch, a lesson you teach, a change you want your team or your family to make.
Just one idea, and hold it in your mind.
First, this week, find the core.
Force yourself to say your idea in one sentence, the way the Army writes Commander's Intent, the way Southwest said THE low fare airline.
If you cannot get it to one sentence, you have not found the core yet, and neither will anyone you are talking to.
Do not add.
Strip away until only the essential thing is left.
Second, catch yourself being the tapper.
This one is a habit, not a task.
The next time you feel that flash of impatience, the why don't they get this feeling, stop, and remember that you have the song in your head and they only hear the taps.
Assume the failure to connect is yours to fix, not theirs.
That single shift in posture will change how you explain everything.
Third, make it concrete.
Take that abstract idea and attach it to something a person can picture.
Find your equivalent of the pomelo, an analogy that borrows a schema they already have.
Or find your fox and grapes, a tiny concrete image that carries the whole lesson.
If your idea currently lives in words like synergy or alignment or value, it is still a bunch of taps.
Give it a body.
Fourth, find your one person.
If you are trying to make people care, do not lead with the statistic, lead with the Rokia.
Find the single, specific, identifiable individual whose story makes people feel the thing you want them to feel.
And be careful about smothering that person in data, because the moment you go analytical, you cool the caring right off.
Fifth, look for the story you already have.
You probably do not need to invent anything.

Somewhere in your world there is a Jared, a real person who lived out the idea you are trying to spread, a springboard story like the Zambian health worker that lets people leap to seeing it for themselves.
Go find it, and tell it, and let it do the work of all six principles at once.
And sixth, hardest and simplest of all, before you deliver your idea, run it past just one other person who does not already know it, and watch their face.
Not for whether they are polite.
For whether the light actually goes on.
Because your own judgment is compromised.
You are cursed.
You know too much.
The only reliable cure is to test your idea on a real listener and find out whether they hear the song, or just the taps.
That is Made to Stick, by Chip and Dan Heath.
The truth it hands you is oddly hopeful.
The reason your best ideas keep dying is not that they are bad ideas, and it is not that you lack some magic gift.
It is that you have never been taught how to make an idea stick, and the people who do it well are not more gifted than you, they are just following a pattern you can now see.
A false story about a stolen kidney has traveled the whole world on its own power, carried by nothing but these six simple traits.
Your true, good, important idea deserves at least that much.
Now you know how to give it wings.
Go find your core, catch yourself tapping, and tell the story only you can tell.
